summary:
The Lycurgan reforms (traditionally c. 650 BC, attributed to the lawgiver Lycurgus) were the comprehensive constitutional and social reorganization of Sparta that created its distinctive militaristic society — establishing the dual kingship, the Gerousia (council of elders), the ephors (elected overseers who checked royal power), and most importantly the social system of common messes (syssitia), land redistribution, and the agoge education system that bound all Spartan male citizens into a single warrior community.

Lycurgus himself may be legendary — Thucydides was skeptical of his historicity — but the 'Lycurgan constitution' was real in its effects. The reforms included the deliberate rejection of wealth displays: Sparta used heavy iron currency useless outside Laconia, forbidding gold and silver accumulation; Spartans ate simple communal meals regardless of personal wealth; and sumptuary laws prevented the competitive display that fueled aristocratic politics elsewhere in Greece. This enforced equality among Spartan citizens (homoioi, 'equals') was extraordinary by ancient standards. The Spartan system was admired by critics of Athenian democracy including Plato (whose Republic draws on Spartan features) and later by various European thinkers who found in Sparta an alternative model of civic virtue over individual freedom.
